# Firestore Schema Design

Our IT Asset Management system uses a simple, flat NoSQL structure in Cloud Firestore.

## Collections

### 1. `assets`
This collection stores all IT assets belonging to Avon Pharmo Chem Pvt Ltd.

**Document ID**: Auto-generated by Firestore
**Document Shape**:
```json
{
  "assetName": "Dell Latitude 5420",
  "category": "Laptop",
  "brand": "Dell",
  "model": "Latitude 5420",
  "serialNumber": "XYZ12345",
  "refNumber": "APC-IT-LAP-001",
  "quantity": 1,
  "unitPrice": 1200.00,
  "totalValue": 1200.00,
  "location": "Head Office",
  "assignedTo": "John Doe",
  "department": "Finance",
  "status": "Assigned", // "Available" | "Assigned" | "Under Repair" | "Retired"
  "condition": "Good", // "New" | "Good" | "Fair" | "Poor"
  "purchaseDate": "2023-01-15T00:00:00.000Z",
  "warrantyEnd": "2026-01-15T00:00:00.000Z",
  "remarks": "Assigned to new finance manager",
  "createdAt": 1673740800000,
  "updatedAt": 1673740800000
}
```

### 2. `users` (Optional - handled by Firebase Auth primarily)
Usually, user accounts are stored in Firebase Auth. However, you can create a `users` collection to store additional user roles (like "Admin"). For this project, we are assuming all logged-in users have access since it's an internal company tool.

## Recommended Security Rules

For a beginner-friendly internal dashboard, we want to allow only authenticated users to read and write to the database.

Go to your Firestore Database -> Rules tab, and paste this:

```javascript
rules_version = '2';
service cloud.firestore {
  match /databases/{database}/documents {
    // Only allow authenticated users to view and edit the assets.
    match /assets/{document=**} {
      allow read, write: if request.auth != null;
    }
  }
}
```
